title: Short-Term Debt and Corporate Governance
abstract: According to existing theories, short-term creditors promote corporate governance by responding quickly to new information. I show that this very feature of short-term debt can also undermine corporate governance. Though moderate levels of short-term debt improve the efficacy of blockholder exit and increase blockholders’ incentives to engage with the firm, high levels of short-term debt impair governance. In particular, high levels of short-term debt render the threat of exit noncredible, make public engagements too risky, and undermine blockholders’ incentives to engage behind the scenes. I identify a challenge in the governance of firms that rely on short-term funding such as banks.

article.1. Related Literature:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Short-term creditors frequently need to decide whether to roll over their claim. As a result, they can react quickly to new information. Indeed, the literature has contended that the ability of short-term creditors to rapidly respond to new information about managerial misbehavior can improve corporate governance (Calomiris and Kahn 1991;Diamond and Rajan 2001). Despite its alleged positive effect on corporate governance, firms primarily funded with short-term debt such as banks seem notoriously famous for weak corporate governance as demonstrated by recurring banking crises.1This paper resolves this puzzle by recognizing the interrelation of short-term creditors and shareholders in corporate governance.2

The basic idea is that because the share price represents a freely available public signal, short-term creditors can use it to guide their rollover decisions. In a model of shareholder governance, short-term debt amplifies the share price reaction to the exit of a large shareholder (blockholder), thereby strengthening its effect on managerial incentives. However, high levels of short-term debt undermine blockholders’ incentives to exit in the first place. The resulting reduction in share price informativeness also undermines blockholders’ incentives to privately engage with firms behind the scenes. Moreover, high levels of short-term debt deter blockholders from initiating public campaigns if success is not guaranteed. As a result, short-term creditors’ ability to respond quickly to new information—the very feature that improves governance in existing theories—can undermine shareholder governance via both exit and voice, and can deprive short-term creditors of the necessary information to discipline management. The model shows that to facilitate exit and voice, firms optimally limit the issuance of short-term debt—even if assets can be liquidated at zero cost. The results can explain why firms that fundamentally rely on high levels of short-term funding such as banks exhibit poor governance.

In the model, a public firm is run by a manager who faces a moral hazard problem and whose payoff is positively associated with the share price. A minority stake of the firm’s shares is concentrated in the hands of a blockholder, who privately observes the state of the firm. Based on her information, the blockholder may exit her position and generate trading profits due to the presence of liquidity traders. The firm has both short-term and long-term debt contracts outstanding among dispersed creditors. Short-term creditors, observing the public share price, must decide whether to roll over their claim after the blockholder has traded.

Given the presence of liquidity traders, the blockholder may want to exit after receiving adverse information so as to sell her shares above the true value. Because the blockholder cannot perfectly camouflage her trade, her exit signals adverse information to the stock market, inducing a decline in the share price. In response, short-term creditors require higher interest rates to roll over their claims. Because higher interest rates reduce the cash flows that shareholders obtain as the residual claimants, the response of short-term creditors amplifies the decline of the share price. The threat of a more severe share price decline upon an exit incentivizes the manager to exert effort. Because the surge in short-term interest rates is anticipated when the blockholder sells, the exit price already reflects the associated decline in shareholder value, making an exit less profitable. As a result, short-term debt may induce the blockholder to exit less frequently and, thereby, incorporate less of her private information into the share price.

For low levels of short-term debt, the expected gains from informed trading exceed the loss in shareholder value accruing from increased interest rates for the blockholder. Thus, the blockholder always exits after obtaining adverse information, yielding a fully informative share price. Because short-term debt only amplifies the share-price movement after an exit (share price sensitivity), managerial incentives to exert effort are improved. For intermediate levels of short-term debt, the anticipated surge in short-term interest rates decreases the exit price too severely so that the blockholder exits less frequently in equilibrium. Increasing the level of short-term debt thus has two countervailing effects for intermediate levels of short-term debt: A higher level of short-term debt renders the share price more information sensitive, improving managerial incentives. However, because the blockholder exits less frequently upon negative news, the share price contains less information on average, thereby dampening managerial incentives. I show that, maybe surprisingly, the reduction in share price informativeness always dominates the increase in share price sensitivity. Hence, for intermediate levels of short-term debt, managerial incentives fall as the level of short-term funding rises. Last, for high levels of short-term debt, the interest rate adjustments are too severe such that exit is no longer profitable, and the blockholder is essentially locked in: the paralyzing effect of short-term debt. This effectively renders the threat of exit empty. Because high levels of short-term debt make the share price too information sensitive, they prevent any information from being revealed in equilibrium. As a result, firms optimally limit the amount of short-term debt, even if premature liquidation is not costly. The optimal debt maturity structure features the highest share price sensitivity that still induces the blockholder to always exit after receiving adverse information.

In practice, shareholders cannot exclusively govern through the threat of exit; they can also intervene directly in the firm’s operations (voice). I extend the model consecutively to consider two different forms of voice: first, private “behind-the-scenes” engagements and, second, public campaigns. Private engagements and monitoring are a cornerstone of blockholder governance (McCahery, Sautner, and Starks 2016). The classical argument regarding the relation of voice and exit (Hirschman 1970) is that private forms of voice are undermined by exit (Coffee 1991;Bhide 1993): A blockholder who can simply exit after negative news (with limited price impact) is less motivated to expend effort ex-ante to prevent it. Hence, one may conjecture that high levels of short-term debt, by committing the blockholder to stay invested, will maximize voice incentives. However, I find that this argument is incomplete: because exit improves share price informativeness, it can also promote voice.

To fix ideas, consider a blockholder who can privately monitor management at a cost. Monitoring, or (private) voice, enhances the likelihood of a high firm value by reducing managerial effort costs and, thus, makes an exit less likely. Because short-term debt reduces exit profits, it has a positive effect on voice incentives. However, blockholder exit also drives share price informativeness. In particular, if the blockholder is expected to exit after receiving adverse information, the absence of an exit conveys positive information. The associated rise in the share price induces short-term creditors to require lower interest rates to roll over, increasing profits after voice and leaving more of the value increase from voice to the blockholder. As long as short-term debt does not induce the blockholder to exit less frequently after bad news, short-term debt thus enhances voice incentives by reducing exit profits and increasing voice profits. Once short-term debt induces the blockholder to exit less frequently, however, there is a countervailing force. If the blockholder exits only sometimes after obtaining adverse information, the absence of an exit conveys less positive information, keeping short-term creditors wary of the firm’s financial state. The resulting less favorable short-term interest rates leave fewer profits from voice to the blockholder. Still, short-term debt decreases exit profits. I show that voice incentives, determined by the payoff difference between voice and exit, decrease for intermediate levels of short-term funding. Intuitively, because debt is repaid more often after good news than after bad news, the less favorable interest rates after voice diminish voice profits to a greater degree than higher interest rates reduce exit profits. For high levels of short-term debt, the blockholder never exits. Hence, the share price and short-term interest rates are uninformative and voice incentives do not change if the level of short-term debt increases further. Governance by exit and (private) voice is thus maximized at the same level of short-term debt, that is, the highest level of short-term debt that is still consistent with blockholder exit (that is, a fully informative share price).

Last, I consider another prominent form of voice: public engagements. Because public engagements reveal information by themselves, exit is not important for share price informativeness to promote public forms of voice. However, the fragility of firms that use high levels of short-term funding may still pose a challenge to public campaigns by activist blockholders. The problem is that the success of public campaigns is highly uncertain in practice.3Because a public campaign signals an underlying problem in the firm, an unsuccessful campaign may induce short-term creditors to update their beliefs negatively about firm value and, thus, demand higher interest rates to roll over. I find that if a public campaign is sufficiently likely to succeed, low levels of short-term debt enhance voice incentives; this is because a successful campaign improves short-term interest rates relative to an exit (which is the relevant deviation for these low levels of short-term debt). By contrast, for larger levels of short-term debt, the blockholder’s best alternative to starting a campaign is to remain passive and invested. As long as campaign success is not certain, this deviation induces lower short-term interest rates. Hence, high levels of short-term debt diminish voice incentives. Public voice incentives are thus also maximized at an intermediate level of short-term debt.

Banks.At least since the global financial crisis in 2008, a central question has been what is unique about banks’ corporate governance (e.g.,Becht, Bolton, and Röell 2011;Mehran and Mollineaux 2012;Laeven 2013). In a recent speech by Andrea Enria, Chair of the Supervisory Board of the ECB, he underscored that “[g]overnance was at the root of the problem”4of recent bank failures.

I identify a novel explanation for why corporate governance in banks is systematically different from that of companies with less short-term funding. While banks’ deposits may be (partially) insured, many deposits exceed the coverage limit5and banks also use a substantial amount of uninsured short-term debt from wholesale funding markets (Adrian and Shin 2010). According to my theory, large shareholders of banks cannot govern by threat of exit because banks’ ample short-term funding renders the threat of exit noncredible. Moreover, if an investor anticipates that she will not be able to unwind her position upon the arrival of adverse private information, this decreases her entry incentives (seeSection 5.2). As a result, short-term debt may hinder block formation. Excessive levels of short-term funding can also undermine voice through behind–the-scenes engagements and public campaigns (Section 3). With high levels of short-term debt preventing governance by exit and voice, there appears to be a vacuum in the corporate governance of banks. This is especially problematic because poor corporate governance reduces firm and shareholder value, thereby exacerbating the cost of issuing scarce equity. Moreover, because high levels of short-term debt prevent block formation, the resulting dispersed ownership can aggravate shareholder-creditor conflicts. Diversified, small shareholders have larger incentives to shift risk than blockholders with concentrated stakes. Consistent with this view,Chen and Duchin (2022)find that short-term funding increases risk-taking.

The theory sheds new light on the established regulatory tools for bank stability. Capital requirements and liquidity coverage ratios have potentially profound effects on bank governance.6While mandatory but limited equity requirements can help by reducing overall leverage, they may not be sufficient to ensure sound governance. If a bank’s debt is predominantly short-term, block formation may not occur. Thus, regulatory measures that limit the amount of short-term debt such as liquidity requirements may facilitate governance by promoting block formation.

The model yields several empirical predictions. Given exogenous variation in the level of short-term debt, the decline in the share price after an exit is predicted to be more pronounced for larger levels of short-term debt. By contrast, share price informativeness ought to decrease in the level of short-term debt. According to the model, firms with very high levels of short-term debt exhibit weaker shareholder governance and a lower ownership concentration.
This paper builds on three strands of the literature: (i) shareholder governance, (ii) short-term debt, and (iii) feedback effects of financial markets. It is the first to study the interaction of short-term creditors and shareholders in corporate governance. FollowingShleifer and Vishny (1986), large shareholders have been studied extensively (early papers include, e.g.,Coffee 1991;Bhide 1993;Maug 1998;Kahn and Winton 1998).Aghion, Bolton, and Tirole (2004)andFaure-Grimaud and Gromb (2004)study the relation of share price informativeness and voice incentives when blockholders need to exit for exogenous reasons. By contrast, I consider an endogenous exit decision, in the spirit ofAdmati and Pfleiderer (2009)andEdmans (2009)to which I add (short-term) debt.

The literature on exit has examined the effect of multiple blockholders (Edmans and Manso 2010), heterogeneous blockholders (Cvijanović, Dasgupta, and Zachariadis 2022), common ownership (Edmans, Levit, and Reilly 2018), and blockholder board representation (Krüger, Limbach, and Voss 2022).Dasgupta and Piacentino (2015)demonstrate that when blockholders are money managers who want to maximize investor flows, the threat of exit loses its credibility. By contrast, in this paper, the feedback effect of short-term debt potentially renders exit unprofitable, even if blockholders maximize trading profits.

Calomiris and Kahn (1991)andDiamond and Rajan (2001)present models in which short-term debt unambiguously improves incentives while introducing the risk of costly premature liquidation. In my framework, short-term debt can directly harm managerial incentives by impairing information revelation in the stock market.Jensen and Meckling (1976);Innes (1990);Boot and Thakor (1993), andEdmans (2011)present different theories showing how debt can improve managerial incentives.Brunnermeier and Oehmke (2014)andLiu (2015)present models in which the anticipation of costly liquidation can lead to excessive short sales.Manso (2013),Goldstein and Huang (2020),Walther and White (2020)andPiccolo and Shapiro (2022)study situations in which creditors learn from credit ratings, policy interventions, and the market price for risk.Opp (2019)uses a dynamic credit-risk model to study the effect of capital injections by an informed blockholder on the (strategic) default of a financially distressed firm.

The survey byBond, Edmans, and Goldstein (2012)stresses that prices affect real decisions through two channels: First, managers learn from the share price to guide (investment) decisions. Second, managers’ compensation contracts and, thereby, their decisions are affected by the share price. This paper clearly focuses on the second channel. An extensive literature studies the feedback effect when managers learn from the share price:Goldstein and Guembel (2008),Bond, Goldstein, and Prescott (2010),Edmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2015),Dow, Goldstein, and Guembel (2017),Davis and Gondhi (2024),Banerjee, Davis, and Gondhi (2021), andMachado and Pereira (2023). Feedback models with other stakeholders includeAlmazan, Chen, and Titman (2017),Ahnert, Machado, and Pereira (2020), andTerovitis and Vladimirov (2022). None of these papers study the debt maturity structure and its effect on governance. Closest in terms of trading mechanism isEdmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2015), who show that speculators may not short-sell if managers take a corrective action based on the share price. While in this paper short-term creditors amplify the share price reaction, they may still undermine trading incentives. The reason is that I consider a blockholder who partially internalizes the negative amplification upon selling so that she may refrain from selling. For an additional discussion of the trading mechanism, seeSection 5.5.Goldstein, Ozdenoren, and Yuan (2013)consider a setting in which equity providers learn from financial market prices and, due to this amplifying action, strategic complementarities among small speculators arise, leading to (inefficient) coordination. The model ofGoldstein, Ozdenoren, and Yuan (2013)does not generate a reduction in trading incentives as the authors consider speculators without prior exposure to the firm. This illustrates that the conjunction of a blockholder and an amplifying action are the two forces that generate the negative effect on trading incentives in this paper. Understanding blockholders’ trading incentives is crucial because they are a cornerstone of corporate governance in otherwise dispersedly held public firms. AsEdmans and Holderness (2017)note: “Blockholders are ubiquitous.[…]It is hard to imagine how firms could survive in a market economy without large shareholders” (p. 542). Moreover, I show that by altering blockholders’ trading incentives, short-term debt affects not only the threat of exit as a central governance mechanism but also blockholders’ incentives to engage in private and public forms of voice. Voice is unique to blockholders because their concentrated stake is necessary to mitigate free-riding.
article.2. The Feedback Effect of Short-term Deb:
2.1. Model

Overview and ownership.There are three periodst∈{1,2,3}and no discounting. A public firm has a continuum of shares of measure one outstanding,α∈(0,12)of these shares are concentrated in the hands of a blockholder(B)⁠. The remaining1−αshares are dispersed among atomistic shareholders. The firm is run by a managerMand invests in a single asset financed by short-term and long-term debt.Mcan increase the asset’s return by a hidden action at a private cost. The blockholder observes a private signal of the firm value and may exit afterward. While long-term debt contracts cover the entire investment horizon, short-term creditors need to roll over. Short-term creditors’ rollover decisions are based on the information contained in the share price.

After investment, the manager may exert hidden efforta∈{0,1}⁠. Att=2⁠, there are two potential statesS∈{SL,SH}of the project. Conditional onSH(SL)⁠, the project’s success probability ispH(⁠pL⁠), wherepHR―>1>pLR―⁠. IfMshirks(a=0)⁠, stateSHrealizes with probabilityq∈(0,1)⁠, whereas working(a=1)increases the probability ofSHbyΔqtoq+Δq≤1⁠. Effort is costly in thatMincurs a privately observable costc∼G[0,c―]⁠, whereG(c)admits a densityg(c)with full support,c―>pHR―andg(c)≤q⁠.7The project has a positive net present value (NPV) ex-ante, even ifa=0⁠; that is,[qpH+(1−q)pL]R―>1⁠. Therefore, the firm always invests in the project.

Debt financing.Att=1⁠, the firm issues debt contracts to outside investors to fund the project. For ease of exposition, debt claims are zero-coupon bonds. There is a unit mass of risk-neutral, perfectly competitive investors, each endowed with a single unit of the numeraire to invest both att=1and att=2⁠. The firm can issue short-term and long-term debt contracts. Letγand1−γdenote the fraction of short-term and long-term debt, respectively. Both debt contracts yield the firm1at issuance. A long-term debt contract covers the entire investment horizon of the long-term project and specifies a face value ofDLTpromised to long-term creditors att=3⁠. By contrast, a short-term debt contract has to be rolled over at the interim datet=2⁠. The initial short-term debt contract determines a face valueDST1promised to investors att=2⁠. Since the company has no liquid funds att=2⁠, the short-term debt contract has to be rolled over by promising short-term creditors face valueDST2att=3⁠. The presence of outside investors att=2ensures that short-term creditors remain perfectly competitive at the rollover date.

If creditors do not invest in the firm, they can simply store their wealth at the risk-free interest rate of zero. If the firm is in need of funds, the long-term project can be liquidated prematurely att=2at the expected project return conditional on all publicly available information. I abstract from the typical early liquidation costs to highlight that short-term debt can be harmful even without exogenously assumed costs. If the project is sold att=2⁠, and the firm cannot honor all debt claims, it defaults, and proceeds are split equally among short-term and long-term creditors. I focus on fundamental runs throughout this paper. As a consequence, if there is no additional information att=2⁠, both short-term and long-term debt induce the same outcome. This allows me to distill the effect of short-term creditors’ response to the share price.

Trading.Att=2⁠, the blockholder privately observes the stateS∈{SL,SH}⁠. Given her private information,Bdecides whether or not to exit her position. In particular,Bchooses the probabilityη∈[0,1]with which to sell her shares to a market maker.8In the tradition ofKyle (1985), liquidity traders simultaneously sell an aggregate amount ofϕor0shares with equal probability. The presence of liquidity traders enables the blockholder to partially camouflage her trade because the market maker only observes the total order flowQ∈Q={−2ϕ,−ϕ,0}⁠. The scope for camouflaging is limited as, prior to trading, the liquidity traders’ selling decision is not observed byB⁠. For ease of exposition, I assumeα=ϕ⁠; that is, the blockholder can unwind her entire stake. The assumption is dropped inSection 5.2.9The market maker, being perfectly competitive, sets the share pricePequal to the expected share value given the total order flowQ⁠.

Informational spillover.At the heart of this paper is an informational spillover from the stock market to short-term creditors. In particular, the rollover decision takes place after trading in the stock market,10such that short-term creditors take the share pricePinto account in their rollover decision. In practice, share prices are easily and freely observable such that even small creditors can use them to become informed. While short-term creditors do not obtain an independent signal in the baseline model, inSection 5.1andInternet Appendix B.1I show that the qualitative results of the model remain unchanged as long as creditors’ own information is not too precise. Intuitively, it is important that creditors can learn additional information from the stock market.

This allows me to stress that the results do not depend on the specifics of the contracting problem.M’spayoff is given by the weighted sum of the share pricePatt=2and the terminal share valueVt=3att=3minus the private effort costc⁠, that is,

2.2. Feedback effect and exit incentives

Since the project has a strictly positive NPV independent of managerial effort, the firm always invests. BecauseM’s payoff from effort is monotone inc⁠, he will exert effort if and only ifc≤c^for somec^∈(0,c―)⁠. Fix an equilibrium conjecturec^and the corresponding probability of the good stateq^∈[q,1)⁠.11I start by analyzing the feedback effect of short-term debt andB’s exit incentives as a function of the firm’s maturity structureγ⁠. Afterward, I characterizeM’s effort choice, the resultingq^⁠, and the optimal level of short-term debtγ⁠.

Informational spillover.When the market maker sets the share price, he anticipates that the short-term creditors condition their rollover decision on the price he quotes. Since debt face values affect payments to shareholders, the market maker will incorporate creditors’ expected response in his pricing rule. The share price is then given by the market maker’s zero-profit condition, which demands that the share price equals the expected shareholder value, that is,

P ( Q ) = E [ m a x { R − γ D S T 2 ( P ( Q ) ) − ( 1 − γ ) D L T ; 0 } | Q ] . (2)

According to the market maker’s break-even condition(2), the share price depends on the total order flow due to the information it conveys about the expected project return. Moreover, the share price decreases in the face values of short-term and long-term debt. WhileDLTis fixed until the final datet=3⁠, the face value of short-term debtDST2(P(Q))is determined att=2and, thus, a function ofP(Q)⁠. Since the share price depends on the face value of short-term debt which, in turn, is a function of the share price,(2)gives rise to a fixed point problem.

The first question, answered by the following lemma, is what short-term creditors can learn from the share price.

Lemma 1. Fix any equilibrium with η ∗ > 0 ⁠ . Then, the equilibrium price function P ∗ ( Q ) : Q → R + is perfectly informative about the realization of the total order flow Q ⁠ .

Rollover.Short-term debt contracts owned by multiple creditors are prone to runs based on coordination failures (Diamond and Dybvig 1983). I focus on fundamental runs. That is, creditors only refuse to roll over their claim if rolling over is a strictly dominated strategy. Consequently, the firm can ensure continuation by offering sufficiently high face values to short-term creditors. Att=2⁠, conditional on observingP(Q)and all other short-term creditors rolling over, a short-term creditor’s break-even condition is

D S T 1 = [ π ( Q ) p H + ( 1 − π ( Q ) ) p L ] D S T 2 ( Q ) . (3)

Note that byLemma 1, creditors’ posterior belief can be directly conditioned onQ⁠. The left side ofEquation (3)is the face value promised to a short-term creditor in the first period. It has to be equal to the expected repayment if the creditor rolls over at face valueDST2(Q)⁠, assuming that the company is continued. Hence, a short-term creditor cannot be induced to roll over if(3)and(4)cannot be jointly satisfied. In this case, the firm defaults and is prematurely liquidated att=2⁠. The proceeds are then split equally among short-term and long-term creditors. Recall that early liquidation is not inefficient, as the project can be sold atE[R|Q]⁠.

The more short-term debt the firm has outstanding, the more debt needs to be rolled over in light of adverse information after an exit. In particular,Q=−2ϕrevealsB’s exit such that creditors know that the expected project return isE[R|SL]<1⁠. Thus, ifγ=1⁠, short-term creditors cannot break even according to(3)and(4). Sufficiently high levels of short-term debt can, hence, induce premature liquidation if the blockholder reveals her private information via an exit. For future reference, letγ^∈(0,1)denote the largest level of short-term debt for which short-term creditors can still be induced to roll over conditional onQ=−2ϕ⁠. In the following, I focus on the equilibria without premature liquidation whenever possible, that is, for anyγ≤γ^⁠.15

The paralyzing effect of short-term debt.Given creditors’ break-even conditions and the market maker’s pricing rule, I now turn toB’s trading incentives. The following assumption ensures that the firm value in stateSLis not too low.

Ifγ<γ―E⁠,c^increases in the level of short-term debt. Short-term debt alleviates the moral hazard problem by making the threat of exit more effective. In particular, more short-term debt implies that both the interim share priceP(Q)and the terminal shareholder valueV(S,Q)react more toB’s exit and, thus,M’s action. Hence, shirking becomes more costly for the manager. Because short-term debt does not undermine share price informativeness forγ<γ―E⁠, short-term debt unambiguously improves managerial incentives.

In general, there are two opposing forces: more short-term debt makes a larger fraction of the company’s debt claims depend on the share price, increasing share price sensitivity. However, more short-term debt also decreases the equilibrium exit probabilityη∗and, thus, share price informativeness.

Asη∗decreases, the marginal benefit of an additional unit of short-term debt decreases because the share price and, thus, short-term interest rates become less informative. Indeed, atγclose toγ―E<1⁠, the marginal benefit of short-term debt approaches zero as short-term interest rates become completely uninformative. By contrast, the equilibrium exit probability and, thereby, share price informativeness decreases linearly inγ(seeFigure 2). Because all inframarginal units of short-term debt become less informative asη∗declines, the marginal cost of short-term debt rises in the level of short-term debt untilγ―E⁠. Therefore, the optimal debt maturity structure has to be belowγ―Eand interior. The optimality of an interior level of short-term debt does not depend on the binary trading technology. Proposition 7 inInternet Appendix B.3establishes in a model with a continuous trading technology that sufficiently large levels of short-term debt can still prevent a blockholder from trading any shares, implying a zero marginal benefit of short-term debt and, thus, an interior optimum.

Maybe surprisingly, I show that the marginal cost of reduced share price informativeness outweighs the marginal benefit of increased share price sensitivity for allγ∈(γ―E,γ―E)⁠. The reason is that the reduction in share price informativeness is endogenously determined by the blockholder’s indifference constraint(5). To keep the blockholder indifferent for rising levels of short-term debt, the reduction inη∗has to be severe enough that the expected payoff from staying invested(ΠNE(η∗))falls.21Hence, asγincreases, the average interest rate conditional on share retention(γ[12DST2(0,η∗(γ))+12DST2(−ϕ,η∗(γ))]+(1−γ)DLT⁠) has to becomelessfavorable. Importantly, because interest rates are solely determined by the order flow, this implies that also in the good stateSH⁠, in which the blockholder will never exit, the average interest rate has to become less favorable asγincreases. Because effort increases the likelihood ofSHbyΔq⁠, managerial incentives are determined by the payoff difference between the two states,SHandSL⁠. Asγincreases, the payoff in both states decreases due to less favorable interest rates so that the question becomes in which stateM’s payoff decreases more severely. The loss due to less favorable short-term interest rates is larger in the good than in the bad state because the company pays back its debt more often in the good state. Intuitively, higher interest payments are more costly if the firm has to actually make them. Thus, the payoff difference between the two states and, thereby, managerial incentives shrink for allγ∈(γ―E,γ―E)⁠.

BecauseBis precisely indifferent between exit and no exit atγ―E⁠, the optimal debt maturity structure is determined by the value ofγat which the upside from potentially selling at inflated prices equals the downside of negative short-term interest rate reversals fromB’s perspective. Therefore,γ―Erises when the upside of selling at inflated prices increases relative to the downside of higher short-term interest rates. As argued above, this upside is maximal whenBcan unwind her entire stake. Ifα>ϕ⁠,γ―Edecreases inαfor a fixedϕ⁠. In the seminal papersCalomiris and Kahn (1991)andDiamond and Rajan (2001), short-term debt unambiguously improves managerial incentives. Short-term debt issuance is only limited because it may lead to costly premature liquidation. By contrast, this model shows that short-term debt can diminish information revelation in financial markets and, thus, directly undermine managerial incentives. As a result, the optimal amount of short-term debt is interior, even when abstracting from costs due to premature liquidation.
article.3. Voice and Exit:
In practice, large shareholders cannot govern only through the threat of exit but also through direct interventions (voice). Following the classical literature on monitoring (e.g.,Holmström and Tirole 1997), I first consider voice as a hidden action of the blockholder. These behind-the-scenes interventions are prevalent in practice and an integral part of blockholder governance (McCahery, Sautner, and Starks 2016). Voice may, however, also frequently take the form of public interventions such as proxy campaigns. After analyzing private interventions, I investigate how public forms of voice are affected by short-term debt. The main result is that excessive levels of short-term debt undermine blockholders’ voice incentives—independent of the form voice takes.

Behind-the-scenes voice.In the context of hidden voice, the literature has shown that a blockholder’s ability to exit can reduce her voice incentives (Coffee 1991;Bhide 1993): if a blockholder can simply escape a low firm value by selling at inflated prices, she has lower incentives to exert effort ex-ante. Since high levels of short-term debt can lock-in the blockholder, one may think that high short-term debt levels maximize voice incentives. However, because exit also provides information to short-term creditors, a complementarity between exit and voice arises, and the voice incentive-maximizing level of short-term debt is again interior. Consistent with this result,McCahery, Sautner, and Starks (2016)present survey evidence among institutional blockholders of “widespread behind-the-scenes intervention as well as governance-motivated exit. These governance mechanisms are viewed as complementarity devices” (p. 1).

Consider a variant of the baseline model ofSection 2.1in which the blockholder can also improve managerial incentives att=1by expending hidden monitoring effortam∈{0,1}⁠. Monitoring is unobservable to investors, dispersed shareholders, and the market maker. Public voice.Another prominent form of voice are public campaigns. Consider the following variant of the model ofSection 2.1. After observing that the firm is in stateS=SLatt=2⁠,Bcan launch a public campaignapub∈{0,1}at a private costκ>0to improve firm value. The public campaign’s outcome is stochastic. With probabilityδ∈(0,1)it succeeds, the firm adopts a new strategy and its state becomesSHfor simplicity. With1−δ⁠, the campaign is not successful and the firm’s state remainsSL⁠. In practice, the success of a proxy campaign is highly uncertain. For instance, an activist blockholder cannot be sure of the support of other shareholders: when it comes to a vote, “there is substantial uncertainty about the voting outcome in these cases: dissidents’ chances of failing or winning a voting contest are about50%” (p. 3,Fos 2017). A reason may be the often reported pro-management bias of, for example, passive funds (Brav et al. 2020;Bubb and Catan 2022;Heath et al. 2022).25More broadly,δ<1captures the idea that voice typically cannot completely undo managerial misbehavior. For instance, because with probability1−δbad investments turn out to be irreversible. In the following, I deriveB’s voice incentives for any fixed equilibrium valuec^∈(0,c―)of managerial incentives.26

Second,Proposition 4shows that voice incentives are humped-shaped in the level of short-term debt, attaining their maximum atγ~⁠. Whenγ≤γ~⁠,B’s best alternative to voice is to exit. In this case, both voice and exit have similar upsides and downsides: with probabilityδvoice succeeds such that firm value becomesV(SH)whereas with probability12exit succeeds in the sense thatBcan camouflage her trade and sell atP(−ϕ)=V(SH)⁠.28Conversely, with probability1−δ⁠, voice fails. Becauseκ―is first decreasing and then increasing in the level of short-term debt, the voice incentive-optimal debt maturity structure isγV∗=γ~⁠. While this may be different from the optimal debt maturity structure for exit and private forms of voice, it is again interior. Thus, excessive levels of short-term debt (⁠γ>max{γ~,γ―V}⁠) impair corporate governance and reduce firm value.
article.4. Empirical Predictions:
The model gives rise to several empirical predictions. Because a firm’s maturity structures is endogenously chosen, testing them requires exogenous variation in the level of short-term debt. Potential sources of exogenous variation include regulatory changes regarding bank funding as well as changes in the coverage limits of deposit insurance.

Given exogenous variation in the level of short-term debt, conditional on blockholder exit, the share price decline should be more pronounced if a company has more short-term debt outstanding. By contrast, the probability of an exit (Section 2.2), or the volume (Section 5.3), should decrease in the level of short-term debt according to the model. These effects are likely to be more pronounced for firms in financial distress for which an exit conveys more information about the probability of default. Note that the unconditional effect on share price volatility is indeterminate, as trade likelihood and price reaction move in opposite directions.

According to the model, blockholders trade less on adverse information for increasing levels of short-term debt. Because blockholders have been identified as a key driver for share price informativeness (Parrino, Sias, and Starks 2003;Bushee and Goodman 2007;Boehmer and Kelley 2009;Brockman and Yan 2009;Gallagher, Gardner, and Swan 2013), the share price is expected to convey less information for larger levels of short-term debt (Section 2).

High levels of short-term debt are detrimental for the threat of exit, behind-the-scenes interventions, and public campaigns. As a result, the model predicts a weaker governance for firms with high levels short-term funding. Further, high levels of short-term debt undermine block formation (measured by 13D and 13G filings) according to the model (Section 5.2).
article.5. Discussion of Assumptions and Extensi:
5.1. Short-term creditors

Information.In the baseline model, only the blockholder and manager have private information regarding firm value. Blockholders, due to the size of their stake, have an incentive to become informed. Empirical evidence shows that their trades provide information that is not already priced in (Parrino, Sias, and Starks 2003;Boehmer and Kelley 2009;Brockman and Yan 2009;Gallagher, Gardner, and Swan 2013;Gorton, Huang, and Kang 2017;Edmans and Holderness 2017). This implies that, in practice, even if short-term creditors also posses some information that they may reveal with their rollover decisions, blockholders add additional information by trading. Evidence also suggests that interest rates in wholesale funding markets do not predict dry-ups, but stock prices do (Pérignon, Thesmar, and Vuillemey 2018).29

InInternet Appendix B.1, I enrich the model by endowing both the blockholder and the short-term creditors with a signal that may reveal the state perfectly or be uninformative. After the blockholder has traded, short-term creditors take their rollover decisions based on the public share price and their private signal. It is then shown that the optimal debt maturity structure is again interior if the likelihood that the short-term creditors are informed is not too large relative to the likelihood that the blockholder is informed. In this sense, the results are robust as long as the blockholder adds enough additional information. By contrast, if short-term creditors were too well informed vis-a-vis the blockholder, the effect of short-term debt changes. At the extreme, if short-term creditors were perfectly informed, short-term interest rates are always fully informative and managerial incentives unambiguously rise inγ⁠. Because blockholders cannot contribute any additional information in this case, blockholder exit does not improve managerial incentives. Well-informed creditors prevent an interior level of short-term debt from being optimal in this extension because creditors and the equity blockholder learn about the same dimension of firm value. Thus, well-informed shareholders prevent the blockholder from adding additional information by assumption. In practice, however, shareholders and creditors may learn about different dimensions of firm value. To see this, consider a richer return distribution in which firm value in case of project failure is not0⁠, butR―<1and uncertain. The (equity) blockholder still learns about the success likelihood whereas creditors, being more concerned with the downside, learn the realization ofR―⁠. Then, an interior level ofγmay still be optimal—even if short-term creditors learnR―perfectly. The reason is that the equity blockholder will still add additional information about the success likelihood with her exit, improving managerial incentives.

The aforementioned empirical research suggests that blockholders contribute additional information by trading. Moreover, there is a theoretical rationale when contemplating blockholders’ and short-term creditors’ incentives to acquire information.

Endogenous information acquisition.While a full analysis of endogenous information acquisition is beyond the scope of this paper, there is a natural complementary in information acquisition by the (equity) blockholder and short-term creditors. When the common prior is that the company is unlikely to default, short-term creditors have little incentives to acquire information: their claim is insensitive to most news in this case because the company can likely repay its debt. By contrast, an (equity) blockholder still has an incentive to acquire information as the value of her claim is more sensitive to information in this case.30If a blockholder acquires information and, despite the low prior probability, observes negative news, she may exit. Her exit, in turn, signals via the stock market to the short-term creditors that the firm has a higher probability of default than initially expected. Given their updated beliefs, it may then be profitable for short-term creditors to acquire additional information. Hence, blockholder exit may be a prerequisite for information production by short-term creditors. According to my theory, this complementarity in information acquisition can only work if companies do not issue excessive levels of short-term debt as these would prevent (equity) blockholder exit and, thereby, undermine short-term creditors’ incentives to acquire information.

Short-term creditors’ identity.In the model, short-term creditors are dispersed and uninsured. In the context of banks, these could be short-term creditors with deposits beyond the coverage limit of the deposit insurance,31or uninsured short-term creditors from wholesale funding markets (Adrian and Shin 2010). In practice, non-banks also typically have multiple short-term creditors (see, e.g.,Ongena and Smith 2000;Brunner and Krahnen 2008). The assumption of dispersed creditors is not crucial for the model, however, because the mechanism does not rely on runs based on coordination failures.

Dual-holdings.Because short-term debt amplifies the efficacy of the threat of blockholder exit, one may wonder whether an equity blockholder has an incentive to also acquire short-term debt claims to coordinate the pressure on management. While this could be socially optimal, it may not be privately profitable for the blockholder. In particular, consider a situation in which the blockholder faces wealth constraints or is risk averse and, thus, only acquires part of the short-term debt contracts.32Then, if the blockholder sells her shares, the share price informs all other short-term creditors and induces them to require higher interest rates or withdraw early. Higher withdrawals by other creditors can be costly to the blockholder as it may prevent her from recovering her own short-term debt investment. Conversely, if the blockholder first refrained from rolling over her debt claims, her private information would be revealed. This, in turn, would prevent the blockholder from realizing trading gains in the stock market.33Overall, reaping the gains from her private information on one claim tends to diminish the possibility of profiting on the other claim, making dual-holdings privately suboptimal.

5.2. Endogenous ownership concentration

By altering trading incentives, the debt maturity structure has important implications for the optimal ownership structure and vice versa. InInternet Appendix B.2, I extend the model by an initial period in which the initial owner sells the firm to a blockholder and dispersed shareholders. The initial owner optimally chooses the block sizeαto maximize firm value and, thus, his proceeds from the sale. The firm value-optimizing ownership concentration maximizes the blockholder’s exit incentives by allowing her to unwind her entire stake upon negative news. Larger stakes force the blockholder to retain part of her shares to camouflage her exit. Hence, the blockholder only profits from selling part of her shares at a price above the fair value but loses on all her shares due to the higher payments to short-term creditors. The blockholder’s trading incentives are, hence, maximized if she can sell her entire stake. Notably, due to the feedback effect of short-term debt, exit profits decrease in the size of her stake, even for a fixed market liquidity.34The optimal level of short-term debt then again allows for the maximal share price sensitivity that does not undermine share price informativeness.

5.3. Endogenous trading volume

InInternet Appendix B.3, I consider a variation of the model that allowsBto freely choose the optimal exit volumeβ∈[0,α]⁠. Consistent with the baseline model, I find that the optimal trading volume decreases in the level of short-term debt and block size. As a result, large levels of short-term debt can undermine exit incentives and share price informativeness in a more flexible model as well.

5.4. Share purchases

Up to now, I have restricted attention to blockholder exit and voice, the most prevalent governance channels in practice. In the absence of risk aversion (Admati, Pfleiderer, and Zechner 1994) and wealth constraints (Winton 1993), the blockholder could also purchase additional shares upon the arrival of positive news. With large levels of short-term debt, the blockholder still does not trade after negative news but buys additional shares after positive news. Hence, short-term debt induces an asymmetric effect on a blockholder’s trading incentives regarding positive and negative information. The overall informativeness of the share price will still decrease in the level of short-term funding as negative news is not incorporated into the share price. For a formal argument, seeInternet Appendix B.4.

5.5. Short selling and the role of the initial stake

According to Inequality(13), shorting itself is profitable despite the negative feedback effect of short-term debt. The reason is that short-term interest rate changes are already reflected in the priceBreceives when shorting and, thus, cancel with the terminal shareholder value. However, a blockholder still needs to bear the surge in short-term interest rates on her initial stake. Intuitively, by trading,Bimposes a negative externality on the other shareholders by increasing short-term interest rates. Independent of whether she sells her shares or engages in short-selling, she internalizes part of the negative effect of the higher debt burden on shareholder value.35Clearly, ifα=0⁠, short-selling is profitable for the “blockholder,” or, in this case, the speculator. Intuitively, if the speculator has no initial stake, she does not internalizes any part of the higher debt burden and, thus, has a strict incentive to sell conditional on adverse information. In practice, however, blockholders with significant stakes are essential because they enhance share price informativeness (Parrino, Sias, and Starks 2003;Bushee and Goodman 2007;Brockman and Yan 2009;Boehmer and Kelley 2009;Gallagher, Gardner, and Swan 2013). Moreover, survey evidence suggests that exit is a key governance channel for blockholders and that “the effectiveness of an exit threat depends on the size of the investor’s equity stake” (p. 2907,McCahery, Sautner, and Starks 2016). As shown inSection 3, the blockholder’s ability to exit upon adverse information also critically affects her incentives to engage in different forms of voice.

The result may be seen as the “inverse” ofEdmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2015), in which a corrective action renders short-selling unprofitable. InEdmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2015), (short)-selling becomes more profitable as the initial stake of the trader increases.36The reason is that inEdmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2015), by using the information contained in the sale, the manager improves overall firm value, raising the value of the initial stake. In contrast, in this paper, short-term creditors reduce shareholder value after an exit by requiring higher interest rates to roll over. Hence, the larger the blockholder’s stake for a fixed stock market liquidity(ϕ)⁠, the more she internalizes the negative effect of higher short-term interest rates.

5.6. Managerial learning

This paper focuses on learning by outsiders from the stock market to isolate its effect on corporate governance. To this end, I allow the blockholder to trade based on an informative signal about managerial effort as is standard in the governance literature (see, e.g.,Holmström and Tirole 1993;Admati and Pfleiderer 2009;Edmans 2009). For blockholders who are in frequent contact with management in practice, trading on retrospective information about managerial effort is a natural starting point. Nonetheless, managers also learn from stock prices to guide their investment decisions (see, e.g.,Chen, Goldstein, and Jiang 2007;Goldstein, Liu, and Yang 2021, for empirical evidence). Allowing for a negative NPV, adding a trading round before the investment and endowing the blockholder with information about the NPV of the project would lead to managerial learning from the stock price to which short-term creditors may also react. In such a model, the stock price would guide investment decisions, influence roll-over decisions, and determine managerial effort with nontrivial interactions among the three. While a formal treatment is beyond the scope of this paper, it may be an interesting avenue for future research.Banerjee, Davis, and Gondhi (2021)point to a potential difficulty when the stock price fulfills two roles, guiding investment decisions and managerial incentives: if it is informative about future investments, it is limited in its informativeness about managerial effort and vice versa.

5.7. Leverage and safe debt

While the focus of the model is the maturity structure of debt, a related question is what role the leverage plays when creditors learn from the share price. To shed light on the impact of leverage, it is useful to relax the simplifying assumption thatR∈{0,R―}⁠, which, as is well-known, cannot capture the relevant differences of debt and equity financing. For instance, a zero return conditional on project failure makes safe debt impossible. Hence, in the model, exit always has an effect on short-term creditors’ required interest rates, independent of the company’s leverage. However, this need not be the case. To see this, supposeR∈{R―,R―}⁠, whereR―>R―>0⁠. In such a setting, debt is safe as long as the company does not issue more of it than it is able to repay even after a project failure, that is,R―⁠. If debt is safe, the fact that short-term creditors learn from the share price does not matter, as they can be fully repaid in any state of the world. Since a higher leverage will tend to make safe debt infeasible, a higher leverage will tend to strengthen the feedback effect of short-term debt.

5.8. Long-term debt rollover and optimal exit timing

A natural question to ask is how the occasional rollover of long-term debt affects governance by exit. Clearly, long-term creditors have as much if not more reason to learn from the share price before rolling over their claim. However, a rollover of long-term debt gives scope for strategic timing of the exit, whereas short-term debt does not. As a consequence, the effect of long-term debt is at most transitory, whereas the effect of short-term debt on exit incentives is persistent. Intuitively, a blockholder can simply postpone her exit until after the rollover date to prevent long-term creditors from reacting to her exit. Conversely, since the defining feature of short-term debt is that it has to be rolled over frequently, the effect of short-term debt on exit is persistent. Whenever the blockholder may exit, short-term creditors face (another) round of rollover decisions shortly afterward. For a formal argument, seeInternet Appendix B.5.
article.6. Concluding Remarks:
This paper shows that, contrary to traditional theories, short-term creditors’ ability to respond quickly to new information can undermine a firm’s governance. Building on the idea that short-term creditors can use the stock price to guide their rollover decisions, I identify a feedback loop between rollover decisions and stock market trading. Given the feedback loop, high levels of short-term debt impair the threat of blockholder exit and blockholders’ incentives to engage with firms behind the scenes as well as their incentives to initiate public campaigns. The theory uncovers a vacuum in the corporate governance of banks: their heavy reliance on short-term funding renders shareholder governance infeasible. As a result, short-term debt undermines the formation of equity blocks which, in turn, may exacerbate shareholder-creditor conflicts since dispersed shareholders have stronger risk-shifting incentives than blockholders.

Narrowly defined, this paper studies the interaction of creditors and blockholder governance. However, more broadly, the underlying forces apply to all stakeholders of the firm, as any stakeholder can use the share price to guide her interactions and contract negotiations with the firm. For instance, employees with career concerns may jump ship after inferring poor growth prospects from the share price. To retain these employees, the firm may need to pay higher wages, reducing shareholder value. Depending on their outside options and switching costs, the employees can either react to the share price or not. These information-sensitive (-insensitive) employees are thus analogous to the short-term (long-term) creditors in this model.

36 Interestingly, the relative profitability of short-selling vis-à-vis buying is independent of the short-seller’s initial stake.
